title: Implement AOT compilation for Maggie bytecode
description:
Compile Maggie bytecode to native code ahead-of-time, eliminating interpreter overhead.

Approach options:
1. Generate Go code from bytecode → compile with `go build` (simplest)
2. Generate LLVM IR → use LLVM backend
3. Generate native assembly directly (most complex)

Recommended: Option 1 (Go codegen) - leverage existing Go toolchain.

For each bytecode method, generate a Go function that performs the same operations directly. The generated code would look like:

```go
func CompiledMethod_Lexer_nextToken(vm *VM, self Value, args []Value) Value {
    // Direct Go code instead of bytecode interpretation
    temp0 := self.GetIvar(0)  // instead of PUSH_IVAR 0
    temp1 := vm.Send(temp0, "peek", nil)  // instead of SEND
    // ...
}
```

Benefits:
- No interpreter dispatch overhead
- Go compiler optimizes the generated code
- Native performance for compiled methods

Deliverables:
- BytecodeToGo translator
- Integration with build process
- Benchmark showing speedup
close_reason: Implemented AOT compiler with bytecode-to-Go translation, VM integration, and benchmarks

title: Native CUE evaluation primitives

Add native CUE evaluation primitives as hand-written Go code (NOT gowrap). CUE is used for procyon-park's workflow engine and requires deep integration with CUE's compiler, module system, and unification engine.

Requirements:
1. CueContext class: create evaluation context, load CUE from string, load CUE from file, load CUE from directory (module-aware with cue.mod/ traversal)
2. CueValue class: validate, unify two values, lookup field by path, iterate fields, convert to Maggie Dictionary/Array/String/Number
3. Schema validation: CueContext validate: data against: schema — returns Success/Failure Result
4. Hidden field injection: ability to inject _input/_ctx hidden fields for workflow step compilation
5. Two-phase compilation support: parse with stubs, then resolve with concrete params
6. Re-compilation: compile CUE with updated context at each workflow step
7. Error handling: CUE validation errors as structured Maggie objects with path and message
8. Full documentation with examples: load and validate, schema checking, workflow config compilation, field extraction
9. Tests: load from string/file/directory, validation pass/fail, unification, hidden field injection, re-compilation with context, error messages
10. Go implementation: vm/cue_primitives.go using cuelang.org/go/cue
11. Maggie lib classes: lib/CueContext.mag, lib/CueValue.mag

title: Implement proper super send in AOT compilation
description:
Implement the OpSendSuper opcode in the AOT (ahead-of-time) compiler, which is currently a TODO stub at vm/aot.go:341.

**Brainstorm ref:** docs/brainstorms/2026-02-02-project-improvements-brainstorm.md — Idea #4 (70% confidence)

**Why:** Super sends are fundamental to Smalltalk — every class that overrides a parent method and calls super relies on this. The AOT compiler can't compile any method that uses super, limiting what it can optimize.

**Files to modify:**
- vm/aot.go (implement super send at line 341, also fix block creation at line 470)
- vm/aot_test.go (add test for super send compilation)

**Relevant source files:**
- vm/interpreter.go (~line 800-850, reference implementation of OpSendSuper)
- vm/compiler_dispatch.go (line 410-412, block method metadata TODOs)

**Key details:**
- Current stub: c.writeLine("// TODO: implement super send properly")
- Super send must look up method in superclass's vtable, not receiver's class
- Smalltalk super semantics: start lookup from DEFINING class's super, not receiver's class's super
- Must track the method's owner class as metadata in AOT-compiled function
- Also fix block creation placeholder at aot.go:470 (currently returns Nil)

**Acceptance criteria:**
- OpSendSuper generates correct AOT code
- Super sends dispatch to parent class methods correctly
- Tests verify super send works through AOT compilation
- Block creation in AOT no longer returns Nil placeholder

title: Thread VM reference through Interpreter
description:
Add a *VM field to the Interpreter struct so that interpreter code can access VM-local registries instead of package-level globals.

## Why
The Interpreter is created per-goroutine (one per fork). It currently has no reference back to its owning VM. This means all registry access from interpreter code (block creation, exception signaling, cell allocation) must use globals. Adding a VM reference unblocks migration of blockRegistry, exceptionRegistry, and cellRegistry.

## What to do
- Add a vm *VM field to the Interpreter struct (vm/interpreter.go)
- Set it during Interpreter creation in NewInterpreter / wherever interpreters are spawned
- Thread the VM through fork paths (vm/concurrency.go fork primitives)
- Do NOT change any registry access patterns yet — just add the plumbing

## Files
- vm/interpreter.go (Interpreter struct, NewInterpreter)
- vm/vm.go (interpreter creation paths)
- vm/concurrency.go (fork creates interpreters)

## Acceptance criteria
- Every Interpreter instance has a non-nil vm field
- All existing tests pass
- No registry access changes (this is pure plumbing)
close_reason: Interpreter.vm changed from interface{} to *VM, type assertions removed

title: Implement block variable captures in Maggie self-hosted compiler
description:
Implement proper variable capture (cell boxing) in the experimental self-hosted Maggie compiler, which currently can't compile blocks that reference outer scope variables.

**Brainstorm ref:** docs/brainstorms/2026-02-02-project-improvements-brainstorm.md — Idea #15 (60% confidence)

**Why:** This is THE blocking issue for Maggie's self-hosting story. Without variable captures, the Maggie compiler can't compile whileTrue: loops, closures that mutate state, or any non-trivial block. Tests explicitly skip these cases.

**Files to modify:**
- Maggie compiler source files (in lib/ or wherever the self-hosted compiler lives)
- cmd/bootstrap/compiler_selfcompile_test.go (un-skip capture tests, lines 308-310)

**Relevant source files:**
- compiler/codegen.go:1045 (Go compiler's capture implementation — reference)
- cmd/bootstrap/compiler_selfcompile_test.go (skipped tests with reason 'Maggie compiler doesn't yet implement proper variable captures')
- cmd/bootstrap/selfcompile_test.go:102-104 (nil return for unsupported features)

**Key details:**
- Three-phase approach matching Go compiler:
  1. Variable analysis pass: scan blocks for outer scope variable references
  2. Cell boxing: allocate captured variables in heap cells instead of stack slots
  3. Capture instructions: generate OpLoadCapture/OpStoreCapture instead of OpLoadLocal/OpStoreLocal
- Must handle nested captures (block inside block capturing outer variables)
- Reference implementation: compiler/codegen.go already does this in Go

**Risks:**
- Complex feature with many edge cases (nested captures, capture of captures)
- Self-hosted compiler already 10-50x slower; capture analysis may worsen this
- Subtle behavior differences vs Go compiler could cause hard-to-debug issues

**Acceptance criteria:**
- Maggie compiler can compile blocks with outer variable references
- whileTrue: loops with mutable state work correctly
- Previously skipped tests in compiler_selfcompile_test.go pass
- Behavior matches Go compiler for all capture patterns
close_reason: Completed by parallel subagents

title: Implement tail-call optimization in interpreter
description:
Implement tail-call optimization (TCO) in the bytecode interpreter so that methods ending with a self-recursive message send reuse the current frame instead of pushing a new one.

**Brainstorm ref:** docs/brainstorms/2026-02-02-project-improvements-brainstorm.md — Idea #8 (65% confidence)

**Why:** Smalltalk relies heavily on recursion (no for loops — iteration is done via message sends like whileTrue:). Without TCO, recursive patterns consume stack frames proportional to iterations. Combined with stack overflow protection, this prevents the stack limit from being a practical obstacle.

**Files to modify:**
- vm/bytecode.go (add OpTailSend opcode)
- compiler/codegen.go (detect tail position, emit OpTailSend)
- vm/interpreter.go (handle OpTailSend by reusing current frame)

**Relevant source files:**
- vm/interpreter.go (current OpSend handling, frame management)
- compiler/codegen.go (expression generation, return detection)

**Key details:**
- Detect tail calls at compile time: a send that is the last expression before a return
- New opcode OpTailSend: instead of pushing new frame, reuse current
- For self-recursive calls: reset frame pc to 0, set new locals, continue
- For non-self calls: fall back to normal send
- Initially only optimize direct self-recursion; mutual recursion is much harder

**Risks:**
- Complicates bytecode and interpreter hot path
- Debugging harder (stack frames disappear)
- May break debugger expectations about stack frames
- Only benefits direct self-recursion initially

**Acceptance criteria:**
- Tail-recursive factorial doesn't grow the stack
- Non-tail calls still create frames normally
- Debugger still works (possibly with reduced stack info for TCO'd calls)
- No performance regression for non-tail calls
close_reason: Completed by parallel subagents

title: Binary operator dispatch ignores .mag-loaded method overrides on String
description:
Binary message sends (e.g. 'abc' < 'abd') return nil for String comparison methods (<, >, <=, >=) even though the methods are correctly compiled and attached from String.mag. The same methods work fine via perform:with: dispatch.

Reproduction:
  ('abc' < 'abd') printString println.          "=> nil"
  ('abc' perform: #< with: 'abd') printString println.  "=> true"
  ('abc' primLessThan: 'abd') printString println.      "=> true"

The docstrings are present (methodDocFor: #< returns the correct doc), so the compiled method IS loaded. The issue is specifically in the binary operator dispatch path — it appears to use a different vtable lookup than keyword/perform: dispatch, and doesn't see methods added after image load.

Discovered while writing doctests for String.mag — had to remove test blocks from <, >, <=, >= docstrings as a workaround.
close_reason: Fixed: two bugs. (1) Binary operator primitives (primitiveLT, primitiveGT, etc.) returned Nil for non-numeric types instead of falling back to VTable lookup. Added sendBinaryFallback method. (2) Interpreter's cached selector IDs were stale after VM replaced the SelectorTable - extracted internWellKnownSelectors() and call it after table replacement.

title: Maggie compiler: implement block variable captures
description:
The Maggie self-hosting compiler (BytecodeGenerator.mag) doesn't implement variable captures for blocks. When a block accesses variables from the enclosing method scope (e.g., [i := i + 1] in a whileTrue: loop), the block can't find those variables and falls back to treating them as globals.

The TODO in visitBlockNode: says 'Handle captures properly'. Need to:
1. Track outer scope temps when compiling nested blocks
2. Use OpPushHomeTemp/OpStoreHomeTemp for method-level temp access from blocks
3. Or implement proper capture mechanism with OpCaptureTemp

This causes whileTrue: loops with variable updates to fail - the loop runs but variable updates don't persist.
close_reason: Fixed by moving selector to method scope and fixing Go compiler's STORE_CAPTURED handling

title: Implement mutable cells for block variable captures
description:
Maggie currently captures block variables by value, not by reference. This breaks standard Smalltalk semantics where mutations to captured variables should be visible in the outer scope.

Example that fails:
```smalltalk
| x |
x := 10.
[x := x + 1] value.
x   "Should be 11, but is 10"
```

Nested block example:
```smalltalk
(test) ifTrue: [
    | count |
    count := 0.
    [count < 3] whileTrue: [count := count + 1].
    ^count   "Should be 3, returns 0"
]
```

Fix requires:
1. Compiler detects variables that are both captured AND assigned
2. Those variables get allocated in heap cells (boxed)
3. All access goes through cell indirection
4. Both Go and Maggie compilers need updating

Current workaround: declare variables at method scope instead of block scope (uses HOME_TEMP opcodes).
close_reason: Implemented mutable cells for block variable captures. Added Cell type to vm/value.go with cellRegistry to prevent GC. Added MAKE_CELL/CELL_GET/CELL_SET opcodes. Updated Go compiler with findCellVariables analysis. Added tests. Maggie compiler has opcode definitions (full analysis TODO).

title: Add stack overflow protection with configurable limits
description:
Add bounds checking to the interpreter's stack push operations and provide a configurable stack depth limit with clear error messages instead of panics.

**Brainstorm ref:** docs/brainstorms/2026-02-02-project-improvements-brainstorm.md — Idea #7 (88% confidence)

**Why:** The interpreter uses a fixed 1024-element stack and 256-frame limit (vm/interpreter.go). No bounds checking on push — deeply recursive Maggie programs cause Go panics instead of Maggie exceptions.

**Files to modify:**
- vm/interpreter.go (add bounds checks on frame push)
- vm/vm.go (add VMConfig with MaxStackDepth, MaxFrameDepth)
- vm/interpreter_test.go (test stack overflow produces clean error)

**Key details:**
- Check bounds on frame push (not every value push — avoids hot path overhead)
- Raise a Maggie-level StackOverflow exception so user code can catch it with on:do:
- Make limits configurable via VMConfig struct
- Default: 1024 stack, 256 frames (preserving current behavior but with protection)

**Design decision:** Prefer Maggie-level exception over Go panic — lets user code handle it gracefully.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- Deep recursion raises StackOverflow exception (catchable with on:do:)
- Limits configurable via VMConfig
- No performance regression on normal code paths (benchmark before/after)
close_reason: Completed by parallel subagents

title: Migrate cellRegistry and weakRefCounter to VM-local
description:
Move cellRegistry and weakRefCounter from package-level globals to VM-local.

## cellRegistry (vm/value.go:304)
- map[*Cell]struct{} — exists solely to prevent Go GC from collecting Cell objects that have been NaN-boxed into uint64 values
- NewCell() registers cells; nothing removes them
- This is a GC root holder, not a lookup table

### Design consideration
Cells are created during block capture (mutable variable cells). They must stay alive as long as any block referencing them exists. Moving to VM-local means cells are released when the VM is GC'd, which is correct behavior.

### What to do
1. Add cells map[*Cell]struct{} + mutex to ObjectRegistry
2. Update NewCell() to accept *VM (or use a VM-scoped factory)
3. This requires all cell creation sites to have *VM access (interpreter has it after task 2)
4. Delete global cellRegistry

## weakRefCounter (vm/weak_reference.go:23)
- atomic uint32 counter for unique weak reference IDs
- Not a registry of objects — just a monotonic counter
- WeakRegistry itself is already per-VM (vm.WeakRefs)

### What to do
1. Move the counter to ObjectRegistry as an atomic.Uint32
2. Update NewWeakReference() to use VM-scoped counter
3. Delete global weakRefCounter

## Acceptance criteria
- No global cellRegistry or weakRefCounter
- Cells are rooted per-VM
- Weak reference IDs are VM-scoped
- All tests pass
close_reason: Migrated cellRegistry and weakRefCounter to VM-local

title: Design review: Non-local returns via panic/recover
description: Questionable design: Non-local returns use panic/recover (interpreter.go lines 229-254, 827-832). While this works, it's slower than a proper return stack and makes debugging difficult. Consider implementing a proper return stack mechanism.
close_reason:
Design Review Complete.

**Current Design Analysis:**
Non-local returns use Go's panic/recover mechanism (interpreter.go:267-297, 974-980):
- NonLocalReturn struct carries the return value and target frame
- Execute() sets up defer with recover to catch non-local returns
- Block return panics with NonLocalReturn, caught by the home method's defer

**Trade-offs:**

Pros of panic/recover approach:
1. **Simplicity**: Leverages Go's existing stack unwinding
2. **Correctness**: Automatically handles arbitrary nesting depth
3. **Interop**: Works correctly across Go/Maggie boundaries (primitives can call blocks)
4. **Safety**: Properly validates home frame is still valid before returning

Cons:
1. **Performance**: panic/recover is ~100-500ns overhead per non-local return
2. **Debugging**: Go stack traces include panic machinery, can be confusing
3. **Profiling**: pprof may show misleading hotspots in recover handlers

**Alternative: Explicit return stack**
A return stack approach would:
- Maintain an explicit "return target" stack alongside the call stack
- Check return targets after each block evaluation
- Avoid panic/recover overhead

**Recommendation: Keep current design**

Reasons:
1. Non-local returns are relatively rare (only explicit ^ in blocks)
2. The 100-500ns overhead is negligible compared to interpreter dispatch
3. Correctness and simplicity outweigh micro-optimization
4. A return stack would add complexity to every block invocation path

**When to reconsider**: If profiling shows non-local return as a hotspot (unlikely), or if implementing a tracing JIT where panic/recover would interfere with optimization.

The current implementation is well-designed with proper goroutine boundary checking and frame validity validation.

title: Multi-VM integration test
description:
Write an integration test that proves two VMs can run independently in a single Go process without interference.

## Test scenarios
1. **Independent channels:** VM-A creates channel with ID 1, VM-B creates channel with ID 1. They are different channels. Sending on one does not affect the other.
2. **Independent processes:** VM-A forks a process, VM-B forks a process. Process IDs may overlap but refer to different processes.
3. **Independent class variables:** Both VMs have class Foo with classVar x. Setting x in VM-A does not affect x in VM-B.
4. **Independent strings:** VM-A creates string "hello", VM-B creates string "hello". They have independent registry entries.
5. **Independent exceptions:** VM-A signals an exception. VM-B is unaffected.
6. **Independent blocks:** VM-A creates a block. VM-B cannot see it.
7. **Independent GC:** RegistryGC on VM-A sweeps only VM-A's registries.

## File
- vm/multi_vm_test.go (new file)

## Acceptance criteria
- All 7 scenarios pass
- Tests run with -race flag without data races
- This is the proof that the migration is complete
close_reason: All 8 multi-VM isolation tests pass (channels, processes, class variables, strings, exceptions, blocks, GC, globals) — clean under -race

title: Epic: Complete migration to fully self-contained VMs
description:
Eliminate all package-level global registries from the vm package so that each VM instance is fully self-contained. This is a prerequisite for running multiple VMs in a single Go process (needed for distributed runtime testing and eventual multi-node operation).

## Current State
The VM has a half-migrated dual-registry architecture:
- ConcurrencyRegistry (VM-local) handles channels, processes, mutexes, waitgroups, semaphores
- 16+ package-level global registries still exist for blocks, exceptions, results, contexts, class vars, cells, strings, dictionaries, gRPC, HTTP, weak refs
- RegistryGC sweeps BOTH systems
- Some tests and the inspector still call global registry functions directly

## Target State
- All object/value registries live on the VM struct (or a sub-struct thereof)
- No package-level var maps in the vm package
- RegistryGC only sweeps VM-local registries
- Two VMs can run independently in one Go process without interference
- All tests pass using VM-scoped registries

## Key Risks
- Block registry is tied to Interpreter (per-goroutine), not VM — needs Interpreter to carry a VM reference
- classVarStorage keys on *Class pointers — if two VMs share class objects, they'd share state (may need per-VM class copies)
- cellRegistry is a GC root holder — moving it to VM changes GC semantics
- String and Dictionary registries are heavily used throughout the codebase
close_reason: All 10 children complete. Multi-VM integration test proves full VM isolation: channels, processes, class vars, strings, exceptions, blocks, GC, and globals are all independent across VMs.

title: Design review: Symbol tags for string/dictionary encoding
description: Questionable design: String, Dictionary, GrpcClient, GrpcStream, Result are all encoded using the symbol tag, requiring cascading checks in vtableFor() (lines 1059-1146 in interpreter.go). Consider using object pointers instead to simplify dispatch logic.
close_reason:
Design Review Complete.

**Current Design Analysis:**
The vtableFor() function (interpreter.go:870-958) uses cascading type checks on symbol-tagged values:
- IsStringValue(v) → String
- IsDictionaryValue(v) → Dictionary  
- isGrpcClientValue(v) → GrpcClient
- isGrpcStreamValue(v) → GrpcStream
- isResultValue(v) → Success/Failure

**Trade-offs:**
1. **Memory efficiency**: Symbol-tagging avoids heap allocation for these values, keeping them as 64-bit NaN-boxed values
2. **Performance cost**: Every dispatch requires cascading type checks (O(n) where n = number of symbol-encoded types)
3. **Complexity**: Adding new symbol-encoded types requires updating multiple dispatch sites

**Recommendation: Keep current design with minor optimization**

Reasons:
1. The types being encoded (strings, dictionaries) are frequently created and benefit from avoiding heap allocation
2. The cascading checks are O(1) in practice (constant number of types)
3. Converting to object pointers would require heap allocation for every string/dictionary creation

**Suggested optimization**: Create a type-tag lookup table at VM init that maps high symbol IDs to vtables, replacing cascading if-else with a single table lookup:

```go
func (i *Interpreter) vtableFor(v Value) *VTable {
    if v.IsSymbol() {
        id := v.SymbolID()
        if vt := i.symbolTypeVTables[id>>48]; vt != nil {
            return vt
        }
    }
    // ... rest of dispatch
}
```

This would be a medium-priority optimization if profiling shows vtableFor as a hotspot.

title: Migrate classVarStorage to VM-local
description:
Move classVarStorage from a package-level global to the VM struct. This is architecturally significant because it determines whether class variables are shared across VMs or independent.

## Current state
- Global at vm/class.go:70 — map[*Class]map[string]Value
- GetClassVar() and SetClassVar() are global functions
- Keyed on *Class pointers — if two VMs share the same *Class objects, they'd share class variable state

## Design decision needed
Two options:
A. **VM-scoped class vars (recommended):** Each VM gets its own classVarStorage. Two VMs with the same classes have independent class variable state. This is the right choice for distribution — each node should have its own state.
B. **Class-scoped class vars:** Store on *Class itself (add a map field). Shared across VMs. Simpler but wrong for distribution.

## What to do (assuming option A)
1. Add classVars map[*Class]map[string]Value + mutex to ObjectRegistry
2. Change GetClassVar/SetClassVar to methods on VM (or ObjectRegistry)
3. Update interpreter opcode handlers (OpGetClassVar, OpSetClassVar) to use interp.vm
4. Delete global classVarStorage
5. Update class_test.go

## Acceptance criteria
- No global classVarStorage
- Two VMs have independent class variable state
- All class variable tests pass

title: Add InjectKey gRPC endpoint for headless UI testing
description:
## Problem

Yutani's current `InjectEvent` gRPC API dispatches events to client event subscriptions, but does NOT inject keystrokes into tview's input queue. This means headless testing cannot simulate actual user keyboard input.

### Current Architecture (Broken for Testing)

```
InjectEvent gRPC → EventDispatcher → Client subscription (Maggie)
                                    ↓
                         MaggieIDE onKey: handler
                         (only handles global shortcuts like Ctrl+Q, Ctrl+I)
```

When testing MaggieIDE:
- `SetProperties` can set text on a TextArea widget ✅
- But Ctrl+Enter to evaluate cannot be simulated ❌
- TextArea doesn't receive character input ❌
- Inspector navigation cannot be tested ❌

### Root Cause

tcell's `SimulationScreen` has an `InjectKey(key Key, r rune, mod ModMask)` method that feeds keystrokes into the screen's event queue, which tview then processes. Yutani creates a SimulationScreen in test mode but doesn't expose this functionality.

## Proposed Solution

Add a new gRPC endpoint `InjectKey` (or rename/extend `InjectEvent`) that calls `SimulationScreen.InjectKey()` when in test mode.

### New Proto Definition

```protobuf
// In event.proto
message InjectKeyRequest {
  SessionId session_id = 1;
  Key key = 2;           // KEY_ENTER, KEY_TAB, etc.
  string rune = 3;       // Character for KEY_RUNE
  repeated Modifier modifiers = 4;  // MOD_CTRL, MOD_SHIFT, etc.
}

message InjectKeyResponse {
  bool success = 1;
  string error = 2;
}

service EventService {
  // ... existing methods ...
  
  // Inject a key into the tview input queue (test mode only)
  rpc InjectKey(InjectKeyRequest) returns (InjectKeyResponse);
}
```

### Server Implementation

In `pkg/services/event.go`:

```go
func (s *EventService) InjectKey(ctx context.Context, req *pb.InjectKeyRequest) (*pb.InjectKeyResponse, error) {
    if !s.server.IsTestMode() {
        return nil, status.Error(codes.FailedPrecondition, "InjectKey only available in test mode")
    }
    
    screen := s.server.GetSimulationScreen()
    if screen == nil {
        return nil, status.Error(codes.Internal, "simulation screen not available")
    }
    
    // Convert proto Key/Modifier to tcell equivalents
    tcellKey := convertKey(req.Key)
    tcellMod := convertModifiers(req.Modifiers)
    var r rune
    if len(req.Rune) > 0 {
        r = []rune(req.Rune)[0]
    }
    
    screen.InjectKey(tcellKey, r, tcellMod)
    
    return &pb.InjectKeyResponse{Success: true}, nil
}
```

In `pkg/server/server.go`:

```go
func (s *Server) GetSimulationScreen() tcell.SimulationScreen {
    if !s.testMode {
        return nil
    }
    if sim, ok := s.screen.(tcell.SimulationScreen); ok {
        return sim
    }
    return nil
}
```

### CLI Support

Add to `yutani debug` or create new command:

```bash
# Inject a single key
yutani inject key -s <session-id> --key KEY_ENTER --mod CTRL

# Inject text (multiple KEY_RUNE events)
yutani inject text -s <session-id> "x := 42"

# Inject key sequence
yutani inject sequence -s <session-id> "x := 42" --then KEY_ENTER:CTRL
```

## Use Case: MaggieIDE Headless Testing

With this feature, we can write automated tests:

```bash
# 1. Start test server
yutani-test-server -address :7756 &

# 2. Start MaggieIDE
mag --yutani --yutani-addr localhost:7756 --ide-tool launcher &

# 3. Get session and TextArea widget IDs
SESSION=$(yutani debug bounds -a localhost:7756 --format json | jq -r '.focused_widget.id')

# 4. Type code
yutani inject text -s $SESSION "x := 42"

# 5. Press Ctrl+Enter to evaluate
yutani inject key -s $SESSION --key KEY_ENTER --mod CTRL

# 6. Verify result in transcript
yutani debug screen -s $SESSION | grep "42"

# 7. Test variable persistence
yutani inject text -s $SESSION "x + 1"
yutani inject key -s $SESSION --key KEY_ENTER --mod CTRL
yutani debug screen -s $SESSION | grep "43"  # Should show 43
```

## Additional Considerations

### InjectMouse

tcell's SimulationScreen also has `InjectMouse(x, y int, buttons ButtonMask, mod ModMask)`. Consider exposing this too for click testing.

### Timing

After injecting keys, callers may need to wait for tview to process them. Options:
1. Add optional `wait_ms` parameter to InjectKey
2. Document that callers should poll screen state
3. Add a `WaitForIdle` endpoint that blocks until tview has processed all pending events

### Security

InjectKey should ONLY work in test mode. The implementation should verify `s.server.IsTestMode()` and reject requests in production mode.

## References

- tcell SimulationScreen docs: https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/gdamore/tcell/v2#SimulationScreen
- Yutani test server: `cmd/yutani-test-server/main.go`
- Current InjectEvent implementation: `pkg/services/event.go:InjectEvent()`
- MaggieIDE event handling: `lib/yutani/ide/MaggieIDE.mag:setupKeyBindings`
close_reason: Already implemented - TestService has InjectKey, InjectText, WaitForIdle, IsTestMode; CLI has yutani inject commands

**Brainstorm ref:** docs/brainstorms/2026-02-02-project-improvements-brainstorm.md — Idea #3 (85% confidence)

**Why:** The parser has 1,410 test cases already, but fuzz testing finds edge cases humans miss — Unicode handling, deeply nested expressions, pathological token sequences, integer overflow in literals.

- compiler/parser.go

**Key details:**

**Acceptance criteria:**

title: Add CI/CD pipeline with GitHub Actions
description:
Add a GitHub Actions CI pipeline that runs all tests, reports coverage, runs go vet and staticcheck, and optionally fuzzes the parser on main branch pushes.

**Brainstorm ref:** docs/brainstorms/2026-02-02-project-improvements-brainstorm.md — Idea #16 (95% confidence)

**Why:** Every other improvement is only sustainable if there's automated testing. Currently tests only run when someone remembers to run go test ./... locally. A CI pipeline catches regressions immediately and tracks coverage over time.

**Files to create:**
- .github/workflows/ci.yml (~60 lines)

**Key details:**
- Run go test -race -coverprofile=coverage.out ./...
- Fail if total coverage drops below 40%
- Run go vet and staticcheck
- Optional fuzz job on main branch (5 min fuzztime)
- Handle missing maggie.image gracefully (some bootstrap tests need it)

**Acceptance criteria:**
- All pushes and PRs trigger test runs
- Coverage reported in CI output
- Builds fail on test failure or coverage regression
- go vet and staticcheck pass cleanly
